findings.title: review_queue asserted as list in one test, assigned a deque in another
findings.evidence: test_init_successful (line 135): assert isinstance(manager.review_queue, list). test_start_session_clears_existing_queue (line 173): review_manager.review_queue = deque([mock_card1, mock_card2]). The field is asserted to be a list in the init test but overwritten with a deque in the clearing test, indicating inconsistent assumptions about the field's runtime type.

findings.title: Disjunctive assertion with always-true exit_code condition renders test vacuous
findings.evidence: Line 967 (TestCoverageGaps.test_review_with_tags): assert '"math" in output or result.exit_code == 0'. A successful CLI invocation always yields exit_code==0, making the right disjunct unconditionally true and the left disjunct (content verification) unreachable as a failure mode. The test passes regardless of whether 'math' appears in output.

findings.title: add_review_and_update_card called with Card object where CardState is expected
findings.evidence: Line 336: db.add_review_and_update_card(sample_review, sample_card) passes a Card as second argument. Lines 464-474 and 509-514 show the same pattern with a Card object passed as new_card_state. All other test files (test_db.py) consistently pass CardState enum values (e.g. CardState.Review) as the second argument. The inconsistency indicates either the API contract is unclear or test_db_errors.py contains incorrect call sites.

findings.title: test_session_with_end_before_start accepts negative duration as valid result
findings.evidence: Lines 274-279: test_session_with_end_before_start sets ended_at before started_at, computes duration_seconds, then asserts duration < 0 with comment '# Duration calculation is based on actual values, not validated'. The test documents that the model produces a nonsensical negative duration without asserting that this is rejected or raising an error, treating a bug condition as an accepted design outcome.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase opened without context manager in review_logic — connection never closed
findings.evidence: review_logic() at line 31 calls db_manager = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path) and then db_manager.initialize_schema() at line 32 and start_review_flow() at line 43, with no 'with' block, no try/finally, and no explicit db_manager.close_connection(). FlashcardDatabase.__exit__ (db/connection.py:106) closes the connection, but __exit__ is never invoked here. The contrasting review_all_logic.py:31 correctly uses 'with FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path) as db_manager:'. If any exception escapes start_review_flow, the DuckDB file descriptor leaks for the process lifetime.

findings.title: db_row_to_review raises pydantic.ValidationError not MarshallingError, bypassing callers' catch blocks
findings.evidence: db_row_to_review at line 157 is 'return Review(**row_dict)' with no try/except. Every caller in database.py (lines 818, 895, 1201) wraps the call in 'except MarshallingError as e: raise ReviewOperationError(...)' but pydantic.ValidationError is NOT a MarshallingError; it escapes that handler, then escapes the surrounding 'except duckdb.Error' block, and propagates uncaught. db_row_to_card (db_utils.py:116-123) and db_row_to_session (db_utils.py:218-222) both properly catch ValidationError and wrap it in MarshallingError, showing the pattern was intentional and this function was omitted.

findings.title: _get_all_due_cards swallows all exceptions and returns empty list, masking DB errors as 'no due cards'
findings.evidence: _get_all_due_cards (line 112) catches 'except Exception as e' at line 156, prints to console, and returns []. The caller (review_all_logic, line 40) treats an empty list as 'No cards are due for review' and exits normally. Any database connection error, schema error, or OOM is silently converted into a clean-exit message. The function also bypasses FlashcardDatabase API by calling db_manager.get_connection() directly (line 128) and executing raw SQL, losing the structured error handling in FlashcardDatabase.get_due_cards().

findings.title: flashcore/yaml_validators.py entire module unreachable from production code
findings.evidence: Grep of all flashcore/**/*.py source files shows no import of yaml_validators from any production module. flashcore/parser.py (the active YAML processing path) imports only from yaml_models (YAMLProcessingError, YAMLProcessorConfig, _FileProcessingContext, _RawYAMLDeckFile) and calls Pydantic's model_validate directly. The validator functions (validate_card_uuid, sanitize_card_text, check_for_secrets, run_card_validation_pipeline, validate_directories, extract_deck_name, etc.) are exercised only from tests/test_yaml_validators.py — absent from any CLI or library call path. The module appears to be a prior architecture that was replaced by the Pydantic-native parser.py approach.

findings.title: start_review_flow never ends the session — every 'review' command leaves end_ts=NULL in DB
findings.evidence: start_review_flow (line 68) calls manager.initialize_session() at line 79, which calls SessionManager.start_session() (session_manager.py:114), which calls db_manager.create_session() persisting a row with end_ts=NULL. After the review loop completes (line 90-125), start_review_flow prints 'Review session finished' and returns without ever calling manager.end_session_with_insights() or manager.session_manager.end_session(). Grep of flashcore/cli/ confirms no end_session calls. db.get_active_sessions() (database.py:1091) returns all rows WHERE end_ts IS NULL, so every finished review session appears permanently active. The review_manager.py:248 method end_session_with_insights exists but is never reachable from the CLI flow.

findings.title: import_from_json in migrate.py has no explicit transaction; partial inserts committed on failure
findings.evidence: import_from_json (line 155) calls _insert_cards then _insert_reviews then _insert_sessions then conn.commit(). No conn.begin() is called before the inserts, and no try/except/rollback guards against partial failure. The finally block at line 202 only calls conn.close(). DuckDB's Python API does not guarantee that executemany is inside an implicit transaction when called on the top-level connection; if _insert_reviews raises (e.g., constraint violation), card rows already executed via _insert_cards may be committed while review rows are missing, producing an inconsistent migration state.

findings.title: _submit_single_review swallows all exceptions and returns None, silently dropping reviews
findings.evidence: _submit_single_review at line 161 wraps the entire ReviewProcessor.process_review call in 'except Exception as e' at line 198, prints to console, and returns None. The caller at line 77 checks 'if updated_card and updated_card.next_due_date' but the None path only prints an error; the review is counted as reviewed (reviewed_count is incremented at line 65 before the call) but no record is persisted to the database. The user is told card N was reviewed when it was not.

findings.title: _ProcessedCardData and _ProcessingConfig dataclasses defined in yaml_models.py but never referenced
findings.evidence: Grep of entire repository for '_ProcessedCardData' and '_ProcessingConfig' shows only one file: flashcore/yaml_models.py (the definitions themselves). Neither dataclass is instantiated or imported anywhere. _ProcessedCardData (line 292) holds uuid, front, back, tags, media, raw_card fields from an earlier design phase; _ProcessingConfig (line 317) mirrors YAMLProcessorConfig but lacks source_directory and fail_fast. Both are entirely inert.

findings.title: end_session_with_insights in ReviewSessionManager is never invoked from any CLI workflow
findings.evidence: Grep of flashcore/cli/ shows zero occurrences of end_session_with_insights. The method generates performance metrics (cards_per_minute, accuracy_percentage, fatigue detection, recommendations, achievements, alerts) and returns a structured dict, but start_review_flow (review_ui.py:68) and review_all_logic (_review_all_logic.py:21) both exit without calling it. The session insights are described as a primary feature in session_manager.py:411 docstring but are never surfaced to the CLI user.

findings.title: backup_database uses naive datetime.now() (local time), inconsistent with UTC convention
findings.evidence: backup_database at line 269 calls 'datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d-%H%M%S")' without a timezone argument, producing a local-time timestamp. Every other timestamp in the codebase uses datetime.now(timezone.utc) (e.g., models.py:97, 101, session_manager.py:148, review_processor.py:92). find_latest_backup (db_utils.py:248) sorts backups by filename, so a DST clock-change (fallback) can produce two files with the same prefix-second, with max() returning an arbitrary one. Using UTC would be deterministic.

findings.title: FLASHCORE_DB env-var fallback in _resolve_db_path is dead code when called from Typer commands
findings.evidence: _db_option (main.py:62) is declared with envvar='FLASHCORE_DB', so Typer reads FLASHCORE_DB and populates db before any command function runs. Therefore when _resolve_db_path(db) is called, 'db is not None' is already true if FLASHCORE_DB was set, and the function returns at line 50. The fallback at lines 51-53 ('env_val = os.environ.get("FLASHCORE_DB")') is unreachable: either db was set by Typer (env or flag) or it is None and the function exits with code 1 without using env_val.

findings.title: Nested _fetch_as_dicts in session_manager._get_session_reviews duplicates database._rows_to_dicts
findings.evidence: _get_session_reviews (line 473) defines a local function _fetch_as_dicts (line 491) that executes a query and returns list-of-dicts using column names from result.description. This is functionally identical to _rows_to_dicts in db/database.py:35-44. The implementation differs only in that it takes a connection and parameters as arguments. The nested function adds indirection and maintenance surface without adding behavior, and is invisible to tests.

findings.title: process_review_by_uuid in ReviewProcessor never called from any production code path
findings.evidence: Grep of flashcore/ for 'process_review_by_uuid' matches only flashcore/review_processor.py (definition) and test files (tests/test_review_processor.py, tests/test_scheduler.py). The method fetches a card by UUID then delegates to process_review(); both the 'review' and 'review-all' CLI paths already hold the Card object and call process_review() directly. The convenience method is tested in isolation but has no caller in the production workflow.

findings.title: flashcore/models.py module docstring is unfilled placeholder
findings.evidence: The module-level docstring at lines 1-3 reads exactly '"""\n_summary_\n"""', which is an IDE-generated placeholder. While not a runtime defect, it violates the stated intent of a disciplined, auditable engineering template and will appear verbatim in generated documentation.

findings.title: Missing timedelta import causes NameError in conftest fixtures
findings.evidence: Line 4 imports `from datetime import date, datetime, timezone` but omits `timedelta`. Fixtures sample_review1 (line 179: `next_due=date.today() + timedelta(days=5)`) and sample_review2_for_card1 (line 198: `next_due=date.today() + timedelta(days=10)`) both reference `timedelta` — any test module that does not redefine these fixtures locally and invokes them will receive NameError: name 'timedelta' is not defined at fixture call time.

findings.title: Inline FlashcardDatabase opened without assignment or close — leaked file handle
findings.evidence: Line 951: `FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path).initialize_schema()` creates a database object that is never assigned to a variable and never closed. The object's destructor is not guaranteed to close the DuckDB file handle promptly, leaving it open for the test's duration and potentially across subsequent tests in the same process.

findings.title: db_readonly opened but never closed in test_read_only_mode_connection
findings.evidence: Lines 344–355: `db_readonly = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path_file, read_only=True)` is opened and used for assertions but no `db_readonly.close_connection()` call or context manager (`with`) is present. The file-backed DuckDB connection is held open until GC runs, which may conflict with other tests or leave a write-lock on the file.

findings.title: db_man connection object not closed — only the raw conn is closed
findings.evidence: Lines 283–293 in `test_instantiation_custom_file_path`: `db_man = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=custom_path)` is created, then the test closes only the low-level `conn` attribute (`db_man.conn.close()`), not `db_man` itself via `db_man.close_connection()`. This leaves the `FlashcardDatabase` wrapper object in an inconsistent state and may leave supplementary handles open.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase db opened without teardown in test_safety_check_with_data
findings.evidence: Line 66: `db = FlashcardDatabase(db_path_file)` is opened for a safety-check assertion and the function exits without calling `db.close_connection()` or using a context manager. The file handle may remain open for subsequent tests.

findings.title: handler_ro ConnectionHandler opened but never closed in test_handle_read_only_initialization_force_recreate_error
findings.evidence: Line 55: `handler_ro = ConnectionHandler(db_path, read_only=True)` is created; the test asserts an error condition and returns without calling `handler_ro.close()` or using a context manager. The underlying DuckDB read-only connection is held open until GC.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase opened without teardown in test_add_review_and_update_card_read_only_mode_raises_error
findings.evidence: Line 413: `db = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=tmp_path / "test.db", read_only=True)` is created; the test exercises the expected exception path but no `db.close_connection()` is called in teardown or a finally block. The file-backed connection leaks until GC.

findings.title: in_memory_db fixtures across six modules lack close_connection teardown
findings.evidence: Fixture `in_memory_db` at line 33 (test_rating_system_inconsistency.py), line 26 (test_review_logic_duplication.py), line 106 (test_review_manager.py), line 24 and line 376 (test_review_processor.py), line 30 (test_session_analytics_gaps.py), and lines 23 and 420 (test_session_manager.py) all return a `FlashcardDatabase(":memory:")` with no `yield`+teardown. Although in-memory DuckDB connections have no persistent file handle, the connection object is not deterministically closed, which may cause inter-test interference within a session.

findings.title: All eight conftest.py fixtures shadowed by identical local definitions in test_db.py — conftest versions are dead code for that module
findings.evidence: Lines 26–206 of test_db.py redefine db_path_memory, db_path_file, db_manager, initialized_db_manager, sample_card1, sample_card2, sample_card3_deck_b, sample_review1, and sample_review2_for_card1 — every fixture also defined in tests/conftest.py (lines 17–205). pytest's local-override rule means test_db.py never exercises the conftest versions. The duplication will silently diverge if either copy is updated independently.

findings.title: Duplicated strip_ansi utility across two CLI test modules with differing regex
findings.evidence: test_main.py defines `strip_ansi()` (line 24) using a multi-pattern regex; test_review_all_logic.py defines `strip_ansi_codes()` (line 21) using the narrower pattern `r"\x1b\[[0-9;]*m"`. The regex difference means the two utilities are not functionally equivalent, introducing potential subtle test differences and maintenance burden when ANSI handling needs updating.

findings.title: Commented-out tests labelled OBSOLETE remain in codebase
findings.evidence: Lines 79–91 contain two fully commented-out test functions (`test_review_logic_uses_default_db_path` and `test_review_logic_creates_db_directory`) with inline docstrings labelling them `OBSOLETE`. They are never executed by pytest and provide no coverage, but consume reader attention and may confuse future maintainers about whether they represent known gaps.

findings.title: Vacuous disjunctive assertions in test_main.py make tests pass trivially
findings.evidence: Lines 912, 944, and 967 each contain assertions of the form `assert "<string>" in output or result.exit_code == 0`. Because the right operand `result.exit_code == 0` is almost always true for a successful CLI invocation, these assertions cannot detect regressions in the specific output strings they nominally check. The disjunction renders the string-presence check vacuous.

findings.title: TestReviewLogicConsolidationRequirements class — pure documentation tests asserting only hardcoded list lengths
findings.evidence: Lines 279–370: three test methods (`test_shared_service_interface_requirements`, `test_backward_compatibility_requirements`, `test_session_integration_future_requirements`) each assert `len(<hardcoded_list>) == N` on lists defined within the test body. They exercise no production code and cannot detect regressions in the actual interface they describe.

findings.title: TestSessionAnalyticsGaps — seven test methods with zero assertions
findings.evidence: Lines 135–348: methods `test_missing_session_analytics_features`, `test_missing_session_lifecycle_management`, `test_missing_cross_deck_session_analytics`, `test_missing_session_performance_analytics`, `test_missing_session_insights_generation`, `test_missing_session_comparison_analytics`, and `test_missing_real_time_session_tracking` contain no assert statements. Each method will always pass regardless of production code state, providing false coverage signal.

findings.title: TestSessionAnalyticsRequirements class — four methods asserting only hardcoded list lengths
findings.evidence: Lines 351–499: `test_data_model_requirements`, `test_api_requirements`, `test_integration_requirements`, and `test_reporting_requirements` each assert `len(<inline_list>) == N`. No production module is imported or called; these tests cannot detect regressions.

findings.title: TestRatingSystemDocumentation — documentation-only tests with no production code assertions
findings.evidence: Lines 321–357: `test_current_rating_scale_mapping` (lines 324–338) verifies an inline dict against itself (`db_scale[db_rating] == meaning` where both dicts are defined in the test body), and `test_fsrs_library_expectations` (lines 340–357) only imports fsrs.Rating and checks `len == 4`. Neither method calls any flashcore production function.

findings.title: FSRS receives zero elapsed days for on-time reviews — scheduling fundamentally broken
findings.evidence: In compute_next_state(), for any non-New card the code sets `fsrs_card.last_review = fsrs_card.due` (line 212), where `fsrs_card.due` is derived from `card.next_due_date`. When the user reviews a card on its due date, py-fsrs computes elapsed_days = review_ts.date() - due.date() = 0, regardless of the card's actual stability (e.g., 10 or 30 days). The local elapsed_days variable (lines 218-224) is also computed as review_ts.date() - last_review.date() = 0 for on-time reviews and is stored in Review.elapsed_days_at_review. FSRS retrievability R = e^(-t/S) with t=0 always equals 1.0, causing the algorithm to treat every on-time review as if no time has passed, producing incorrectly short or long next intervals. The Card model has no last_review_at datetime field, so the cached due date is the only proxy used, but it represents the scheduled target date, not the actual prior review timestamp.

findings.title: Review queue re-sorted by modified_at clobbers FSRS-optimal due-date ordering
findings.evidence: initialize_session() calls self.db.get_due_cards() which returns cards ordered by 'next_due_date ASC NULLS FIRST, added_at ASC' (database.py:459). Immediately after, line 109 re-sorts the returned list: `self.review_queue = sorted(due_cards, key=lambda c: c.modified_at)`. modified_at is updated to datetime.now(UTC) on every review (database.py:703). After the first review of any card, that card moves to the end of future queues. New (never-reviewed) cards are prioritized by ingestion-time order, not FSRS overdue priority. This overrides the scheduler's intended card ordering and breaks the spaced-repetition contract.

findings.title: Deck switch counting undercounts because previous_deck tracks last new deck, not last reviewed deck
findings.evidence: In record_card_review(), `previous_deck = self.deck_access_order[-1] if self.deck_access_order else None` (line 205-206). deck_access_order only appends a deck the first time it is seen (line 209: `if card.deck_name not in self.deck_access_order`). So after the sequence A,B,A,B: iteration 4 (second B card) finds deck_access_order=[A,B], previous_deck=B (last appended), card.deck_name=B, so B==B — no switch counted. But the actual transition was A→B, which is a real switch. The counter reports 2 switches for A→B→A→B when the correct count is 3. Also, the Session.add_card_review() method (models.py:364) has its own independent deck_switches logic; using both causes double-tracking inconsistency (session_manager.py:213-217 manually increments deck_switches on current_session without calling add_card_review()).

findings.title: Path traversal check uses str.startswith() — directories sharing a name prefix bypass containment
findings.evidence: In validate_single_media_path(), after resolving the candidate path: `if not str(full_media_path).startswith(str(abs_assets_root)):`. If abs_assets_root resolves to '/home/user/assets' and an attacker supplies a relative path that resolves to '/home/user/assets2/evil.png', then str.startswith('/home/user/assets') evaluates to True (the prefix 'assets' matches the beginning of 'assets2'), allowing the path outside the assets root to pass the containment check. The correct idiom is Path.is_relative_to() (Python 3.9+) or checking startswith(str(abs_assets_root) + os.sep).

findings.title: vet_logic uses non-recursive glob; misses YAML files in subdirectories
findings.evidence: When no explicit files_to_process are given, vet_logic collects: `list(source_dir.glob('*.yml')) + list(source_dir.glob('*.yaml'))` — these patterns match only the top-level directory. In contrast, load_and_process_flashcard_yamls() in parser.py:285-288 uses `source_directory.rglob('*.yaml') + source_directory.rglob('*.yml')`, which descends into subdirectories. Any deck YAML file stored in a subdirectory of source_dir will be ingested (parser path) but silently skipped by vet (vet_logic path), leaving those files unvetted and potentially without UUIDs.

findings.title: deck_switch_efficiency has no lower bound and can return negative values
findings.evidence: _calculate_performance_metrics() computes: `'deck_switch_efficiency': round(100 - (session.deck_switches * 10), 1)`. With 11 or more deck switches the result is -10 or lower. focus_score on line 627 correctly applies `max(..., 0)` to avoid negative values, but deck_switch_efficiency lacks this guard. A negative efficiency metric is semantically invalid and will surface to callers of generate_session_insights() as a numeric anomaly.

findings.title: timedelta not imported in conftest.py but used in two shared review fixtures
findings.evidence: Line 5 imports `from datetime import date, datetime, timezone` — `timedelta` is absent. Lines 180 and 202 reference `timedelta(days=5)` and `timedelta(days=10)` directly inside the `sample_review1` and `sample_review2_for_card1` fixtures. Any test that requests either fixture fails at collection time with `NameError: name 'timedelta' is not defined`. Both fixtures are shared via conftest.py and are transitive dependencies for tests across test_db.py, test_review_manager.py, test_review_processor.py, and test_scheduler.py.

findings.title: review_all_logic test asserts 1 card reviewed when submission raised an exception
findings.evidence: At line 181, `mock_submit.side_effect = Exception('Database error')` makes submission raise before returning. Yet line 190 asserts `'Review session complete! Reviewed 1 cards.' in clean_output`. A card whose submission threw should not increment the reviewed counter; this assertion either documents or masks incorrect error-handling logic where a failed submission is counted as a completed review. Line 189 asserts the error message is printed, confirming the exception is caught, which makes counting the card doubly wrong.

findings.title: Contradictory deck_switches expectation for return-to-visited-deck across two test files
findings.evidence: test_session_model.py line 147 asserts `session.deck_switches == 1` after the sequence A→B→A (with comment 'No additional switch (deck already accessed)'). test_session_manager.py line 177 asserts `session.deck_switches == 2` after the identical sequence Math→Science→Math (with comment 'Should now have 2 switches: Math -> Science -> Math'). The two test files encode mutually exclusive business rules for the same scenario; exactly one is wrong. Both are currently in the passing test suite, meaning the production code satisfies one and the other passes only because it tests a different code path (Session.add_card_review vs ReviewSessionManager.record_card_review).

findings.title: review_queue type invariant contradicted: list asserted in init test, deque assigned in queue-clear test
findings.evidence: TestReviewManagerInitialization.test_initial_state asserts `isinstance(manager.review_queue, list)` at line 134, establishing that the queue is a list. TestStartSessionAndGetNextCard.test_start_session_clears_existing_queue at line 171 then pre-populates the queue with `review_manager.review_queue = deque([...])`, assigning a `collections.deque` to the attribute just asserted to be a list. If ReviewSessionManager.initialize_session uses list-only operations (e.g. slicing, index assignment) the deque assignment masks type-dependent bugs in the cleared-queue path. The import of `deque` at the test file header (verified from context) confirms the assignment is intentional.

findings.title: Negative session duration accepted and asserted as correct graceful handling
findings.evidence: test_session_with_end_before_start (lines 270-279) constructs a Session with `end_ts = start_ts - timedelta(minutes=5)` and asserts `duration < 0` with comment 'Negative duration indicates error condition'. The test docstring says 'Duration calculation should handle this gracefully', yet a raw negative result is not graceful handling — it is an unguarded arithmetic outcome. Any downstream analytics that divide by duration (e.g. cards_per_minute) will produce negative or nonsensical values. The test encodes a specification that silently propagates invalid state rather than clamping to 0, raising ValueError, or returning None.

findings.title: Six gap-documentation tests in TestSessionAnalyticsGaps contain only comments, no assertions — always pass
findings.evidence: Lines 154-349 contain six test functions (test_missing_session_lifecycle_management, test_missing_cross_deck_session_analytics, test_missing_session_performance_analytics, test_missing_session_insights_generation, test_missing_session_comparison_analytics, test_missing_real_time_session_tracking). Each executes real DB writes via manager.initialize_session() or upsert_cards_batch() but contains zero assert statements — only '# GAP:' comment lines. All six pass unconditionally regardless of whether the documented gaps exist or have been closed. Real DB side-effects are incurred with no coverage signal, creating non-deterministic state in the test run.

findings.title: test_current_rating_scale_mapping documents deprecated 0-3 UI scale as current behavior after 1-4 unification
findings.evidence: TestRatingSystemDocumentation.test_current_rating_scale_mapping (line 324) is titled 'Document the current rating scale mappings' and constructs `ui_scale = {0: 'Again', 1: 'Hard', 2: 'Good', 3: 'Easy'}` at line 327, asserting `db_rating == ui_rating + 1` as the conversion formula. The companion test test_ui_rating_scale_is_1_to_4_after_fix within the same file (line 63, class docstring line 322 states 'for future reference') confirms the fix unified ratings to 1-4. The 0-3 scale and DB=UI+1 formula represent the deprecated pre-fix behavior. The test encodes and labels the obsolete scheme as 'current', which will mislead developers auditing the rating path. The intent_mismatch is true because this stale documentation directly contradicts the stated goal of rigorous, accurate change governance.

findings.title: Path Traversal Bypass in Media Path Validation — startswith Flaw
findings.evidence: Line 267: `if not str(full_media_path).startswith(str(abs_assets_root)):` uses string prefix matching rather than a proper path-prefix check. A media path of `../cards_other/secret.txt` (relative, passes the is_absolute guard at line 249) resolves via `resolve()` to a sibling directory, e.g. `/data/cards_other/secret.txt`, which satisfies `startswith('/data/cards')` because the sibling name shares the prefix. The correct guard is `full_media_path.is_relative_to(abs_assets_root)` (Python 3.9+) or `str(full_media_path).startswith(str(abs_assets_root) + os.sep)`. Current code stores the escaped path in the DB without detecting the boundary violation.

findings.title: Supply Chain Risk — Mutable VCS Dependency Installed in CI
findings.evidence: Line 33: `pip install "aiv-protocol @ git+https://github.com/ImmortalDemonGod/aiv-protocol.git"` installs from the default branch of an external repository with no commit SHA pin. Any push to that repository's default branch immediately changes the code executed in the aiv-guard CI job. The installed package is subsequently run as `python -m aiv.guard` (line 38) with access to the checked-out source and any CI secrets present in the environment. A compromised or hijacked package version could exfiltrate secrets, tamper with validation results, or inject code into the CI pipeline.

findings.title: Overly-Broad CI Token Permissions (`permissions: write-all`)
findings.evidence: Line 6: `permissions: write-all` grants the `rename-project` job every GitHub token permission including write access to code, issues, pull-requests, packages, deployments, and repository metadata. Combined with the force-push at line 44 (`push_options: --force`) and the `stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action` at line 39, any code injection in this workflow step would have full repository write authority. The principle of least privilege requires scoping permissions to only what is needed (e.g., `contents: write`).

findings.title: GitHub Actions Expression Injection — Unquoted Context Variables in Shell
findings.evidence: Line 37: `.github/rename_project.sh -a ${{ env.REPOSITORY_OWNER }} -n ${{ env.REPOSITORY_NAME }} -u ${{ env.REPOSITORY_URLNAME }} -d "Awesome ${{ env.REPOSITORY_NAME }} created by ${{ env.REPOSITORY_OWNER }}"` — the `${{ env.* }}` expressions are expanded inline into the shell command before the shell sees them and are not quoted. If `REPOSITORY_NAME` or `REPOSITORY_OWNER` contain shell metacharacters (spaces, semicolons, backticks, `$(...)`) — which can arise from repositories with unusual names — the shell would interpret them as commands. GitHub hardening guidance requires quoting `${{ }}` interpolations or passing them through env vars.

findings.title: Unpinned Third-Party Action Tags — Mutable SHA Risk
findings.evidence: Multiple workflows reference third-party actions by mutable version tags rather than immutable commit SHAs: `codecov/codecov-action@v5` (main.yml:62), `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` (release.yml:29), `stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action@v7` (rename_project.yml:39). A mutable tag can be silently redirected to a different commit without any diff visible in this repository, enabling arbitrary code execution in CI with whatever permissions the workflow grants. GitHub security hardening guidance requires pinning to a full commit SHA.

findings.title: End-of-Life Python Base Image in Containerfile
findings.evidence: Line 1: `FROM python:3.7-slim` — Python 3.7 reached end-of-life on 2023-06-27 and no longer receives CVE patches or security releases. Any vulnerability discovered in the CPython interpreter, the standard library, or the bundled pip after that date will remain unpatched in containers built from this image. The project targets Python 3.10/3.11 in CI (main.yml lines 21-22) but ships a 3.7 image.

findings.title: Unquoted Shell Variables and Unescaped sed Patterns in rename_project.sh
findings.evidence: Lines 26-29: `sed -i "s/$original_author/$author/g" $filename` — (a) `$filename` is unquoted, causing word-splitting and glob expansion on filenames containing spaces or shell metacharacters; (b) `$author`, `$name`, `$urlname`, `$description` are inserted directly into the sed `s/…/…/g` pattern without escaping. A value containing `/` breaks the sed delimiter; a value containing `&` or `\1` is reinterpreted as a back-reference. Values containing newlines or shell metacharacters could cause arbitrary command execution in the sed invocation context. These values originate from `OPTARG` (attacker-controlled if the script is called with user-supplied arguments).

findings.title: GHA Shell Injection Pattern — Direct PR SHA Expansion Without Quoting
findings.evidence: Line 207: `git diff ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}...${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }} -- 'tests/**/*.py'` — PR SHA values from the GitHub event payload are interpolated directly into the shell command without quoting. While GitHub constrains SHA values to [0-9a-f]{40} in practice, this pattern nonetheless violates the GitHub Actions hardening guideline of never using `${{ }}` directly in `run:` blocks, and a logic bug or context pollution could introduce non-hex characters that break or inject into the command.

findings.title: Bleach Allowlist Permits Arbitrary External URLs in `img[src]` and `a[href]`
findings.evidence: Lines 92-95: `DEFAULT_ALLOWED_HTML_ATTRIBUTES` permits `img[src]` and `a[href]` with no URL-scheme restriction. Card front/back text sanitized by bleach retains `` verbatim. If cards are rendered in a browser (current or future Anki/Markdown export), external image loading enables pixel tracking and side-channel data exfiltration. There is no confirmed server-side renderer that would make this a direct SSRF; impact is at the browser/client level. Additionally, `a[target]` is permitted without enforcing `rel="noopener"`, enabling reverse-tabnapping when `target="_blank"` links are followed.

findings.title: Incomplete HTML sanitization exposes XSS vector in card text
findings.evidence:
test_sanitize_card_text at line 277 asserts front == "

Question

alert('xss')" — only

findings.title: Autouse fixture prepends tmpdir to sys.path for all tests
findings.evidence: go_to_tmpdir (autouse=True) calls sys.path.insert(0, str(tmpdir)) at line 23 before yielding. Any file placed in the test tmpdir whose name collides with a stdlib or application module will shadow the real import for the duration of every test in the session. In a contaminated build environment this is a module-injection vector.

findings.title: Path traversal via symlink not tested — coverage gap
findings.evidence: test_validate_single_media_path_traversal_attack at line 98 confirms '../outside_assets.jpg' is blocked. No test exercises a symlink inside the assets root that points outside it. Symlink escape is a standard bypass for literal-path traversal guards; absence of a symlink test leaves this protection unverified.

findings.title: FLASHCORE_DB environment variable accepted as raw filesystem path without validation
findings.evidence: test_resolve_db_path_from_envvar at line 886 sets FLASHCORE_DB to an arbitrary tmp_path string and invokes the CLI. No test asserts that the path is validated against an allowlist or constrained to a known directory. In shared or container environments an attacker who can set environment variables can point the database at any writable path.

findings.title: Secrets detection test only exercises the bypass flag, not the detection path
findings.evidence: test_secrets_detection_flag at line 344 asserts that skip_secrets_detection=True processes the secret-bearing YAML without errors. The complementary assertion — that skip_secrets_detection=False (the default) rejects the same YAML — is absent. The secret-detection code path is exercised in test_yaml_validators.py but not against the card-level parser fixture that contains the placeholder secret string at line 82.

findings.title: Placeholder secret value present in test fixture YAML
findings.evidence: YAML_WITH_SECRET_CONTENT defined at line 82 contains a placeholder secret value in the 'a' field of a card entry (category: placeholder API key string, already anonymised as REDACTED_STRIPE_KEY_FOR_TESTS). The value is intentional test data, not a real credential leak, but confirms the fixture was authored around a real-looking secret pattern that the secrets detector is expected to catch.

findings.title: Containerfile specifies Python 3.7 image — package requires Python >=3.10, container cannot run installed code
findings.evidence: Line 1: `FROM python:3.7-slim`. pyproject.toml line 11 declares `requires-python = ">=3.10"`. pip will refuse to install the package into a Python 3.7 interpreter with 'requires Python >=3.10'. Any container built from this Containerfile is non-functional for running flashcore. CI (main.yml) tests against Python 3.10 and 3.11, making the container the only artifact that contradicts the declared requirement. Python 3.7 also reached end-of-life in 2023-06-27.

findings.title: Anki export command is a permanent placeholder stub — declared intent feature not implemented
findings.evidence: Lines 543-549: `@export_app.command("anki") def export_anki(): """Export flashcards to an Anki deck (Not implemented).""" console.print("[yellow]Export to Anki is not yet implemented. This is a placeholder.[/yellow]")`. The provisional intent explicitly lists 'exporting to Anki/Markdown' as a core feature of the engine. Markdown export is implemented in `flashcore/cli/_export_logic.py:export_to_markdown`. The Anki export command is registered in the CLI but emits only a warning message and performs no actual export. No corresponding implementation file (e.g., `_export_anki_logic.py`) exists anywhere in the codebase.

findings.title: release.yml calls legacy `python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel` — contradicts pyproject.toml-based build system
findings.evidence: Line 51: `python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel`. The project uses a modern setuptools/pyproject.toml build configuration (pyproject.toml line 1: `[build-system]`, line 3: `requires = ["setuptools>=61", "wheel", "setuptools_scm"]`). The canonical build command for PEP 517/518 projects is `python -m build`. .taskmaster/tasks/task_008.md references a subtask to 'deprecate/rename setup.py' (Task 1.3). The release workflow bypasses the modern build backend, which may produce incomplete or incorrect distributions (e.g., missing metadata that pyproject.toml provides) and contradicts the documented project structure.

findings.title: yaml_validators.py defines a full card validation pipeline that parser.py never calls — dead parallel implementation
findings.evidence: yaml_validators.py defines `run_card_validation_pipeline` (line 315), `validate_directories` (line 365), `extract_deck_name` (line 398), `validate_raw_card_structure` (line 508), and several helper validators — a complete parallel validation pipeline. parser.py imports none of these: it calls `_RawYAMLDeckFile.model_validate()` directly for deck validation (parser.py uses pydantic-model-direct approach) and handles card processing inline. A search across all flashcore/*.py and flashcore/cli/*.py confirms zero production imports of yaml_validators symbols. yaml_validators is only imported in tests/test_yaml_validators.py. The module is tested but functionally disconnected from the production code path, creating maintenance debt: changes to yaml_validators have no effect on actual YAML processing.

findings.title: Makefile release target commits directly with `git commit`, bypassing mandatory AIV commit workflow
findings.evidence: Line 84: `@git commit -m "release: version $${TAG} 🚀"`. CLAUDE.md documents that 'every commit on this project must go through `aiv commit`, not plain `git commit`' and provides a full AIV commit workflow with risk tiers, falsifiable claims, and packet generation. The Makefile `release` target uses plain `git commit`, bypassing AIV entirely. Any release commit produced via `make release` will lack the required AIV packet, violating the change governance invariant and potentially failing the `aiv-guard.yml` CI check.

findings.title: CI workflows reference actions/checkout@v6, setup-python@v6, upload-artifact@v6 — version tags do not exist
findings.evidence: main.yml references `actions/checkout@v6` (lines 25, 43, 75, 103), `actions/setup-python@v6` (lines 26, 44, 76, 104), `actions/upload-artifact@v6` (lines 53, 85). aiv-guard.yml lines 31-32 also reference `checkout@v6` and `setup-python@v6`. As of the audit date, the latest released versions of these actions are checkout@v4, setup-python@v5, upload-artifact@v4. Version tag v6 does not exist in any of these action repositories. GitHub Actions resolves missing tags with a 'tag not found' error, meaning all jobs in main.yml and aiv-guard.yml fail at the checkout/setup step. This renders the entire CI pipeline non-functional.

findings.title: .windsurfrules DEV_WORKFLOW section triplicated verbatim; Node.js tooling references not applicable to Python project
findings.evidence: The `## DEV_WORKFLOW` section header appears at lines 9, 535, and 1062; each occurrence is followed by an identical ~370-line block (verified: grep -c 'DEV_WORKFLOW' = 3). The triplicated block instructs developers to run `npm install -g claude-task-master`, `node scripts/dev.js <command>`, and `npx task-master` — none of which are valid in this Python project (no package.json, no scripts/dev.js, no Node.js runtime configured). The default PROJECT_NAME in the environment documentation section is `"MCP SaaS MVP"`. These are residual artifacts from an upstream template, not project-specific documentation, and the triplication creates a 1400-line file that misleads developers about the actual toolchain.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabaseError defined in exceptions.py but never imported or raised anywhere
findings.evidence: exceptions.py lines 64-66 define `class FlashcardDatabaseError(DatabaseError): """Raised for errors specific to the FlashcardDatabase operations.""" pass`. A search of all flashcore/*.py, flashcore/cli/*.py, flashcore/db/*.py confirms zero imports and zero raises of `FlashcardDatabaseError`. The class is defined but has no callers, making it dead code that creates a false expectation that database errors surface under this type.

findings.title: _CardProcessingContext, _ProcessedCardData, _ProcessingConfig dataclasses defined in yaml_models.py but unused in production
findings.evidence: yaml_models.py defines `_CardProcessingContext` (line 265), `_ProcessedCardData` (line 292), and `_ProcessingConfig` (line 318) — dataclasses evidently designed for the yaml_validators.py pipeline. yaml_validators.py imports `_CardProcessingContext` and `_ProcessedCardData` from yaml_models, but since yaml_validators.py is itself not called by parser.py (see D005), these classes have no production execution path. `_ProcessingConfig` is not imported even by yaml_validators.py — it has zero references outside yaml_models.py itself. The dataclasses represent scaffolding for a validation approach that was superseded without cleanup.

findings.title: .taskmaster/config.json sets projectName to 'Taskmaster' instead of 'flashcore'
findings.evidence: Line 28: `"projectName": "Taskmaster"`. The repository is named 'flashcore', the pyproject.toml name is 'flashcore', and CLAUDE.md is authored as the flashcore project guide. The taskmaster config retains the upstream tool's own name rather than the project's identity. Any task-master CLI output or generated reports that surface projectName will incorrectly identify the project.

findings.title: Makefile switch-to-poetry target hardcodes wrong project name 'a_flask_test' and template author 'rochacbruno'
findings.evidence: Line 101: `@poetry init --no-interaction --name=a_flask_test --author=rochacbruno`. This target would initialize a Poetry project with the wrong name ('a_flask_test') and a template author identity ('rochacbruno') rather than the actual project name ('flashcore') and actual maintainer. Lines 119-122 in the same file confirm the rochacbruno/python-project-template origin. Running `make switch-to-poetry` would silently create an incorrectly named pyproject.toml.

findings.title: _review_logic.py opens FlashcardDatabase without a context manager — connection leak risk
findings.evidence: Line 31: `db_manager = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path)` — the connection is stored in a local variable and the function proceeds without `with db_manager:`. If an exception occurs anywhere after this line, the connection is never closed. `_review_all_logic.py` (the sibling module) correctly uses `with FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path) as db_manager:` as a context manager. The inconsistency between the two review CLI entry points creates a subtle resource-leak risk under error conditions.

findings.title: .aiv.yml lists 'scripts/' as a functional_prefix — contradicts CLAUDE.md architecture rule
findings.evidence: Line 13 of .aiv.yml includes `"scripts/"` in the `functional_prefixes` list, causing AIV to treat changes in flashcore/scripts/ as functional commits requiring R1+ risk tier with full packet generation. CLAUDE.md explicitly states: 'Scripts (flashcore/scripts/): utility scripts, NOT part of the installed package. Never import from flashcore.scripts in core code.' pyproject.toml line 59 confirms: `exclude = ["tests", ".github", "flashcore.scripts"]`. The governance configuration treats scripts as core functional code while the architecture documentation classifies them as peripheral utilities.

findings.title: .github/init.sh hardcodes rochacbruno template URLs — template artifact active in project tooling
findings.evidence: Line 35: `template_url="https://github.com/rochacbruno/${template}-project-template"`. This script, if executed, would download and apply another rochacbruno project template on top of the existing flashcore codebase. The script is a residual from the python-project-template origin and has no valid use case in the deployed flashcore project. Its presence in .github/ means it may be executed inadvertently by maintainers exploring template tooling.

findings.title: flashcore/models.py retains '_summary_' placeholder module docstring from template
findings.evidence: Line 3: `"""_summary_"""`. This is the unfilled template placeholder generated by IDE/template tooling for module-level docstrings. The models.py module defines core domain types (Card, CardState, Session, Review, etc.) and is one of the most important modules in the codebase, but its docstring describes nothing. All other modules in the package have meaningful or empty docstrings; this one uniquely retains the placeholder.

findings.title: dump_history.py interpolates table name directly into SQL via f-string
findings.evidence: Line 45: `conn.execute(f"SELECT * FROM {table_name}")`. `table_name` is drawn from an internal list in the same function, not from user input, so this is not an exploitable injection path in the current code. However, f-string SQL interpolation is a structural anti-pattern: if the call site ever changes to accept external input, this becomes a SQL injection. The rest of the codebase uses parameterized queries consistently (database.py uses `?` placeholders throughout).

findings.title: requirements-test.txt duplicates pyproject.toml test dependencies without version pinning
findings.evidence: requirements-test.txt lists test dependencies (pytest, pytest-mock, etc.) independently of pyproject.toml's `[project.optional-dependencies]` test group. The two lists may diverge silently: a dependency added to pyproject.toml but not requirements-test.txt (or vice versa) will produce different test environments depending on which file is used to install. requirements.txt itself contains only `-e .` and a comment pointing to pyproject.toml as the source of truth, but requirements-test.txt is not consistent with that approach.

findings.title: Dead code: two OBSOLETE-labelled tests remain commented out
findings.evidence: Lines 79-83 and 86-90 contain two commented-out test functions each prefaced with a docstring reading 'OBSOLETE: This test is no longer valid…'. They are inert dead code: they execute nothing, produce no coverage signal, and cannot be un-commented without error because the logic they targeted was removed. Their presence inflates the apparent completeness of the file.

findings.title: Anki-export test asserts placeholder message — codifies stated goal as unimplemented
findings.evidence: test_export_anki_stub (line 697) invokes the 'export anki' CLI command and asserts exit_code==0 and output contains 'Export to Anki is not yet implemented. This is a placeholder.' The provisional project intent explicitly lists Anki export as a primary deliverable. A passing test that validates a 'not implemented' stub gives false CI green on an undelivered feature.

findings.title: 'Documentation' test encodes pre-fix 0-3 UI rating scale as 'current' behavior
findings.evidence: test_current_rating_scale_mapping (line 324) declares ui_scale = {0: 'Again', 1: 'Hard', 2: 'Good', 3: 'Easy'} and asserts only that the conversion formula db = ui + 1 holds for each pair. The module docstring (lines 1-12) states 'Bug: The system uses two different rating scales: UI: 0-3 … DB: 1-4.' The unified post-fix Rating enum (flashcore/models.py:32-40) defines Again=1 through Easy=4; the 0-3 UI scale no longer exists. The test hardcodes the broken pre-fix scale, passes trivially (arithmetic identity), and misleads maintainers about the current rating contract.

findings.title: Stale TODO claims ReviewProcessor is yet to be built — it already exists
findings.evidence: Line 211: `assert True  # This will be fixed by creating a shared service`. The ReviewProcessor class is fully implemented and imported at the top of tests/test_review_processor.py. The comment describes a future state that has already been delivered, leaving future readers to doubt whether the refactoring was actually completed.

findings.title: TestReviewLogicConsolidationRequirements tests assert only hardcoded list lengths — always pass
findings.evidence: Three methods in TestReviewLogicConsolidationRequirements (test_shared_service_interface_requirements line 283, test_backward_compatibility_requirements line 328, test_session_integration_future_requirements line 351) build plain Python lists of requirement strings and assert only their lengths (e.g., assert len(required_interface['parameters']) == 8). No live code is invoked. These tests were authored as pre-implementation design specs; they pass regardless of whether ReviewProcessor was built correctly, incompletely, or not at all.

findings.title: Module docstring describes session-analytics gaps that same-file tests assert are already fixed
findings.evidence: The module docstring (lines 4-12) states 'ReviewSessionManager has session_uuid but doesn't create Session objects', 'No session lifecycle management in review workflows', and 'No session analytics or insights generation'. Lines 59-133 of the same file contain test_review_session_manager_now_creates_session_objects and test_review_workflows_now_have_session_integration, both annotated '# Gap is FIXED!' with assertions that verify Session objects are created and analytics are tracked. The docstring describes the pre-fix state and directly contradicts the tests below it.

findings.title: Six 'test_missing_*' tests contain only setup code and GAP comments — no assertions
findings.evidence: test_missing_session_lifecycle_management (line 154), test_missing_cross_deck_session_analytics (line 180), test_missing_session_performance_analytics (line 227), test_missing_session_insights_generation (line 263), test_missing_session_comparison_analytics (line 294), and test_missing_real_time_session_tracking (line 326) each contain setup calls and only '# GAP: …' comment blocks. Zero assert statements appear in any of them. They pass unconditionally whether or not the 'missing' feature is absent or fully implemented, providing no coverage signal.

findings.title: add_review_and_update_card called with Card object in one test and CardState enum in another
findings.evidence: Line 336 calls `db.add_review_and_update_card(sample_review, sample_card)` where sample_card is a Card instance (constructed at lines 319-330). Line 391 calls `db.add_review_and_update_card(sample_review, CardState.Review)` where the second argument is a CardState enum value. The two call-sites make contradictory assumptions about the method's second-parameter type, indicating either the signature is ambiguous or one test is incorrect about the expected contract.

findings.title: review_queue asserted as list in one test and assigned as deque in another
findings.evidence: Line 134: `assert isinstance(manager.review_queue, list)` (in TestInit.test_initialization). Line 171-172: `review_manager.review_queue = deque([Card(...)])` (in TestStartSessionAndGetNextCard.test_start_session_clears_existing_queue). The first test declares the queue must be a list; the second test writes a deque into it without error, implying the implementation accepts either type. These two tests encode incompatible assumptions about the concrete type of a core data structure.

findings.title: Comment says Rating.Easy corresponds to value 3 — actual value is 4
findings.evidence: Line 502: `rating = Rating.Easy  # Corresponds to value 3`. flashcore/models.py:40 defines `Easy = 4`. The comment is a holdover from the pre-fix 0-3 UI scale era; under the unified 1-4 Rating enum, Easy is 4, not 3. A reviewer reading this test would deduce the wrong value for the Easy rating.

findings.title: Docstring for sample_card3_deck_b contains malformed UUID missing one hyphen group
findings.evidence: Line 148 of the docstring reads: 'The card has UUID "33333333-3333-3333-333333333333"' — this string has only three hyphen-delimited groups before the last segment and is not a valid UUID-4 format (should be 8-4-4-4-12). The Card constructor at line 154 uses the correct value '33333333-3333-3333-3333-333333333333'. The docstring would mislead anyone trying to grep for this card by UUID.

findings.title: Four conftest.py fixtures redefined verbatim in test_db.py — shadow without override
findings.evidence: tests/test_db.py defines db_path_memory (line 25), db_path_file (line 36), db_manager (line 50), and initialized_db_manager (line 84) — identical in logic and signature to the same four fixtures in tests/conftest.py:30-93. Module-scoped fixture redefinitions silently shadow the conftest versions for tests in test_db.py. Any future divergence between the two copies (e.g., teardown logic, parametrize sets) would affect only one module, creating silent behavioral inconsistency.

findings.title: Skipped test references removed testing_mode mechanism without documenting replacement path
findings.evidence: @pytest.mark.skip(reason='Requires testing_mode mechanism - deferred until new implementation') and the inline comment 'that was removed during DI refactoring. Skip until reimplemented.' The skip reason names a mechanism that was removed; there is no pointer to what would replace it or any tracking issue. test_initialize_schema_force_recreate (line 390) is a no-op `pass` body. Without either a live assertion or a TODO pointing to a follow-up ticket, this test will remain silently skipped indefinitely.

findings.title: Deck-switch count for A→B→A is 1 in Session model tests but 2 in SessionManager tests
findings.evidence: tests/test_session_model.py:147: `assert (session.deck_switches == 1)  # No additional switch (deck already accessed)` for the navigation pattern Deck A → Deck B → Deck A. tests/test_session_manager.py:177: `assert session.deck_switches == 2` with comment 'Should now have 2 switches: Math -> Science -> Math' for the identical A→B→A pattern. Session.add_card_review() and SessionManager.record_card_review() implement different deck-switch-counting semantics and both are tested as correct. Any session analytics derived from deck_switches will produce inconsistent values depending on which path updates the counter.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase connection never closed in review_logic
findings.evidence: Line 31 instantiates `db_manager = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path)` without using it as a context manager. Line 32 calls `db_manager.initialize_schema()` which opens the underlying DuckDB connection. The function returns after `start_review_flow` completes without ever calling `db_manager.close_connection()` or using `__exit__`. `FlashcardDatabase` has no `__del__`, so the connection is leaked until the interpreter GC finalizes the DuckDB native object. The contrast is explicit: every other DB call site (`_review_all_logic.py:31`, `main.py:276`, `main.py:424`, `main.py:581`) uses `with FlashcardDatabase(...) as db_inst:`.

findings.title: db_row_to_review does not wrap ValidationError in MarshallingError, breaking error-handling contract
findings.evidence: The function body is `return Review(**row_dict)` with no try/except. Every caller in `database.py` catches `MarshallingError` to wrap it in a domain error (e.g., `database.py:821 except MarshallingError`, `database.py:899 except MarshallingError`, `database.py:1204 except MarshallingError`). `db_row_to_card` (db_utils.py:116-123) and `db_row_to_session` (db_utils.py:217-223) both catch `ValidationError` and re-raise as `MarshallingError`. `db_row_to_review` does not, so a malformed review row raises an uncaught `pydantic.ValidationError` that escapes both the inner `except MarshallingError` and the outer `except duckdb.Error` guards, propagating as an unhandled exception to the CLI.

findings.title: Entire yaml_validators module is dead code — never imported in production
findings.evidence: The only import of `yaml_validators` anywhere in the repo is `tests/test_yaml_validators.py:5: from flashcore.yaml_validators import (...)`. No file under `flashcore/` imports it: `grep -r 'from .yaml_validators\|from flashcore.yaml_validators' flashcore/` returns zero results. `parser.py` imports only from `yaml_models` and uses `_RawYAMLDeckFile.model_validate()` and its own `_prepare_card_data`/`_process_single_raw_card` methods. All 15 functions in `yaml_validators.py` (`validate_card_uuid`, `sanitize_card_text`, `check_for_secrets`, `compile_card_tags`, `_handle_skipped_media_validation`, `_validate_media_path_list`, `validate_media_paths`, `validate_single_media_path`, `run_card_validation_pipeline`, `validate_directories`, `extract_deck_name`, `extract_deck_tags`, `extract_cards_list`, `validate_deck_and_extract_metadata`, `validate_raw_card_structure`) are exercised by tests but unreachable from any production entry point.

findings.title: HTML sanitization via bleach bypassed in production ingestion pipeline
findings.evidence: Consequence of DEAD-001: `yaml_validators.sanitize_card_text` (yaml_validators.py:64) calls `bleach.clean()` on card `q`/`a` fields to strip disallowed HTML. However, because `parser.py` never imports `yaml_validators`, the production ingestion path (`YAMLProcessor._prepare_card_data`, parser.py:144-162) does only `card_data['front'] = card_data.pop('q')` and `card_data['back'] = card_data.pop('a')` — raw strings pass directly into `Card(**card_data)` and then into the database without any HTML sanitization. Cards containing `<script>` or other payload HTML are stored verbatim. Similarly, `yaml_validators.check_for_secrets` (yaml_validators.py:93) for secret detection is also bypassed.

findings.title: _ProcessedCardData dataclass defined but never used
findings.evidence: The dataclass `_ProcessedCardData` (yaml_models.py:292) is defined with fields `uuid`, `front`, `back`, `tags`, `media`, `raw_card`. `grep -r '_ProcessedCardData' flashcore/` returns only the definition line — no instantiation, no import, no reference in any production or test file.

findings.title: _ProcessingConfig dataclass defined but never used
findings.evidence: The dataclass `_ProcessingConfig` (yaml_models.py:318) with fields `assets_root_directory`, `skip_media_validation`, `skip_secrets_detection`, `fail_fast` has zero references outside its definition. `grep -r '_ProcessingConfig' flashcore/` returns only the definition. It is distinct from the used `YAMLProcessorConfig` (yaml_models.py:307).

findings.title: FlashcardDatabaseError exception class never raised in production code
findings.evidence: `FlashcardDatabaseError` is defined as a subclass of `DatabaseError` (exceptions.py:64). `grep -r 'FlashcardDatabaseError' flashcore/` shows no usages in production code. It appears only in test files: `tests/cli/test_main.py:18` (imported) and lines 357, 544, 627, 790 (used as mock side_effect to simulate errors in tests). The production exception hierarchy uses `CardOperationError`, `ReviewOperationError`, `SessionOperationError`, etc., never `FlashcardDatabaseError`.

findings.title: _get_session_reviews duplicates get_reviews_for_session bypassing its error handling
findings.evidence: `SessionManager._get_session_reviews` (session_manager.py:473) manually gets a raw connection via `self.db_manager.get_connection()` (line 489), executes raw SQL `SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE session_uuid = $1` (line 508), and manually constructs `Review` objects from row dicts (lines 521-553). This duplicates `FlashcardDatabase.get_reviews_for_session` (database.py:1174-1214) which does the same query with proper error handling (`except duckdb.Error`, `except MarshallingError`). The handrolled version: (a) silently swallows all exceptions via `except Exception` at line 557 returning `[]`, hiding database errors; (b) duplicates UUID conversion logic (lines 524-536) that `db_utils.db_row_to_review` handles; (c) does not benefit from future changes to `get_reviews_for_session`.

findings.title: backup_database uses naive local datetime, inconsistent with UTC convention
findings.evidence: `datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')` (db_utils.py:269) uses the local system clock with no timezone. Every other timestamp in the codebase uses UTC: `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` (models.py:97, 100, review_manager.py:62, session_manager.py:148, etc.). Backup file ordering by name (`max(backup_files, key=lambda p: p.name)` at db_utils.py:248) is correct only when all timestamps use the same timezone; local-time names would sort incorrectly when DST transitions occur or when the machine timezone changes.

findings.title: Redundant initialize_schema call inside context manager in review_all_logic
findings.evidence: `with FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path) as db_manager:` at line 31 invokes `__enter__` (database.py:110-121), which calls `self.initialize_schema()` when `is_new_db` is True. Line 32 then explicitly calls `db_manager.initialize_schema()` unconditionally. For a new database this triggers schema DDL twice; for an existing database the context manager skips the call but line 32 forces an unnecessary schema SQL execution on every `review-all` invocation. The SQL is idempotent (`IF NOT EXISTS`) so data is safe, but the extra transaction and DDL round-trip is wasteful and does not occur in the `export md` or `stats` commands.

findings.title: export anki command is an unimplemented stub — intent mismatch with stated Anki export goal
findings.evidence: The `export_anki` function (main.py:543) prints `'Export to Anki is not yet implemented. This is a placeholder.'` and returns. The provisional intent explicitly lists 'exporting to Anki' as a goal. The CLI registers this as `flashcore export anki` but the command performs no work. No implementation exists in `_export_logic.py` either. The contrast with `export_to_markdown` (which is fully implemented) confirms this is an incomplete feature.

findings.title: Two fully commented-out test functions are dead code retained in the file
findings.evidence: Lines 79-90 contain two commented-out test stubs: `# def test_review_logic_uses_default_db_path():` (line 79) and `# def test_review_logic_creates_db_directory(tmp_path):` (line 84). Both have docstrings that remain visible but neither is ever executed by pytest. The comment `# OBSOLETE:` in each docstring signals these were intentionally disabled but never removed. Dead comment-only code with no production coverage value.

findings.title: Disjunctive assertion `'No flashcards found' in output or result.exit_code == 0` is unfalsifiable when exit code is 0
findings.evidence: Line 912: `assert 'No flashcards found' in output or result.exit_code == 0`. Because the CLI exits 0 on success the right branch is always True for any passing run, making the left branch untestable. The assertion passes even if the expected message is absent, turning this into a tautology. The same pattern appears at lines 944, 967, and 1044 in the same file.

findings.title: Disjunctive assertion `'already exist' in output or result2.exit_code == 0` is unfalsifiable when exit code is 0
findings.evidence: Line 944: `assert 'already exist' in output or result2.exit_code == 0`. When the CLI exits with code 0 the right-hand disjunct is True regardless of whether `output` contains 'already exist'. The test will always pass on any successful invocation, providing no guarantee that the duplicate-deck warning message is actually emitted.

findings.title: Disjunctive assertion `'math' in output or result.exit_code == 0` is unfalsifiable when exit code is 0
findings.evidence: Line 967: `assert 'math' in output or result.exit_code == 0`. The right-hand branch is True whenever the CLI exits cleanly, so the assertion never forces the word 'math' to appear in the output. A regression that silently drops the deck tag from the output would not be caught by this test.

findings.title: Disjunctive assertion `'cancelled' in output.lower() or result.exit_code == 0` is unfalsifiable when exit code is 0
findings.evidence: Line 1044: `assert 'cancelled' in output.lower() or result.exit_code == 0`. The right-hand branch is True for any zero-exit-code run, making the cancellation-message check untestable. A regression that removes the 'cancelled' confirmation message would not be detected by this assertion.

findings.title: Missing `timedelta` import in conftest.py causes NameError risk in shared fixtures
findings.evidence: Line 5: `from datetime import date, datetime, timezone` — `timedelta` is absent. Lines 182 and 201 use `timedelta(days=5)` and `timedelta(days=10)` inside the `sample_review1` and `sample_review2_for_card1` fixtures. Any test that uses these conftest fixtures (rather than a per-file shadow) will raise `NameError: name 'timedelta' is not defined` at runtime. Tests that define local fixtures with the same names silently mask the bug.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase `db_readonly` created with read_only=True never closed in test_read_only_db_write_attempt
findings.evidence: Lines 942-949: `db_readonly = FlashcardDatabase(db_path_file, read_only=True)` is instantiated and used to verify that write attempts raise `CardOperationError`, but no `db_readonly.close_connection()` call and no context manager is used. The DuckDB read-only connection is leaked after the test completes. Other tests in the same file consistently use `with FlashcardDatabase(...) as db:` or explicit `close_connection()` teardown.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase `db` never closed in test_safety_check_with_data
findings.evidence: Lines 62-72: `db = FlashcardDatabase(db_path_file)` is created, used to insert cards and trigger a `ValueError`, but no `db.close_connection()` is called and no `with` block is used. The database connection remains open after the test exits. The pattern is inconsistent with `test_force_recreate_tables_with_empty_db` in the same file which uses `with FlashcardDatabase(...) as db:`.

findings.title: pytest.raises(Exception) catch is too broad in test_upsert_error_handling_with_closed_connection
findings.evidence: Line 111 uses `with pytest.raises(Exception):` which accepts any exception including `SystemExit`, `KeyboardInterrupt` (after Python 3.8 they inherit from `BaseException` but `Exception` still catches broad errors), `AssertionError`, `NameError`, and others. The test intends to verify that writing to a closed database raises a database-specific error. Using a domain exception such as `CardOperationError` or at minimum `duckdb.Error` would falsify the correct failure mode rather than silently masking unrelated errors.

findings.title: FlashcardDatabase `db` opened in read_only=True mode never closed in test_add_review_and_update_card_read_only_mode_raises_error
findings.evidence: Lines 409-432: `db = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=tmp_path / 'test.db', read_only=True)` is instantiated to verify that adding a review raises an error in read-only mode. The test has no `finally` block, no context manager, and no explicit `db.close_connection()`. The DuckDB file descriptor remains open after the test. Contrast with other tests in the same file that use `with FlashcardDatabase(...) as db:` to ensure connection teardown.

findings.title: No-op `assert True` in test_code_duplication_analysis is a guaranteed-pass dead assertion
findings.evidence: Line 211: `assert True  # This will be fixed by creating a shared service`. An unconditional `assert True` can never fail and provides zero coverage of production code. It is a placeholder left after the intended assertion was removed. Any test runner will always report this test as passed regardless of the state of the codebase.

findings.title: test_code_duplication_analysis asserts only lengths of locally-defined Python lists — no production code exercised
findings.evidence: Lines 167-211: the test body constructs three local Python lists (`duplication_steps`, `common_parameters`, `review_fields`) that are hand-typed inside the test, then asserts `len(duplication_steps) == 6`, `len(common_parameters) == 4`, `len(review_fields) == 12`. No import from `flashcore` is invoked. The assertions can never fail unless someone edits the test itself; they validate nothing about the production codebase. The test serves only as inline documentation.

findings.title: TestRatingSystemDocumentation class tests only local Python dicts — zero production code exercised
findings.evidence: Lines 321-357 contain two tests (`test_current_rating_scale_mapping`, `test_fsrs_library_expectations`). `test_current_rating_scale_mapping` (line 324) builds two hand-coded dicts `ui_scale` and `db_scale` and verifies the arithmetic `db_rating = ui_rating + 1`. `test_fsrs_library_expectations` (line 342) imports `fsrs.Rating` and asserts `len(fsrs_ratings) == 4`. Neither test exercises any `flashcore.*` production logic path; both would pass even if the entire `flashcore` package were deleted.

findings.title: test_missing_session_lifecycle_management has zero assertions after initialize_session — always passes
findings.evidence: Lines 153-179: after `manager.initialize_session()` is called at line 169, the remaining lines 171-179 are exclusively `# GAP:` comments with no `assert` or `pytest.fail` statements. The test as a whole always passes regardless of session lifecycle state because there are no falsifiable assertions following the setup.

findings.title: test_missing_cross_deck_session_analytics has zero assertions — always passes
findings.evidence: Lines 181-226: the test inserts math and science cards then contains only `# GAP:` comment blocks. There are no `assert` statements at all in the test body. `pytest` reports this as a passing test unconditionally. The test is a placeholder that documents missing features without enforcing any contract.

findings.title: test_missing_session_performance_analytics has zero assertions — always passes
findings.evidence: Lines 227-262: after submitting five reviews via `manager.submit_review()`, the remainder of the test body is entirely `# GAP:` comments with no `assert` or `pytest.fail`. The reviews are submitted (exercising production code) but no analytic output is checked. The test always passes and cannot catch regressions in session performance tracking.

findings.title: test_missing_real_time_session_tracking has zero assertions — always passes
findings.evidence: Lines 327-349: the test calls `manager.initialize_session()` then contains only `# GAP:` comments with no assertions. No post-initialization state is verified. The test trivially passes on every run and contributes no regression detection for real-time session tracking behavior.

findings.title: FSRS elapsed_days computed from due date instead of actual last-review date
findings.evidence: Line 212 sets `fsrs_card.last_review = fsrs_card.due` (the scheduled next-due date), not the date of the last actual review. The elapsed-days computation at lines 219-224 then calculates `elapsed_days = review_ts.date() - fsrs_card.last_review.date()`, i.e. days since the scheduled due date, not days since the last actual review. For any card reviewed exactly on its due date, elapsed_days = 0 regardless of the true inter-review interval (which could be 7, 30, or 90 days). FSRS uses elapsed_days in its forgetting-curve R(t)=e^(-t/S); returning 0 every on-time review makes FSRS believe recall is 100% at the moment of each review, systematically underestimating forgetting and distorting the stability update formula. The in-code comment ('Set last_review to due date for elapsed_days calculation') confirms this is intentional, but the semantic mismatch with what the FSRS algorithm expects makes scheduling incorrect for all non-overdue reviews.

findings.title: deck_access_order[-1] freezes after last unique deck is added, undercounting deck_switches
findings.evidence: In `SessionManager.record_card_review`, `previous_deck` is set from `self.deck_access_order[-1]` (line 205-207), which only contains unique decks appended in first-seen order. Once all decks are in the list the list tail is frozen. Example: reviews A, B, A, B — after step 2, deck_access_order=['A','B']; step 3 (review A): previous='B', B≠A so deck_switches increments correctly. Step 4 (review B): previous='B' again (deck_access_order[-1]='B' unchanged because B is already in list), so the A→B switch at step 4 is never detected and deck_switches stays at 2 instead of 3. Every subsequent switch back to the last-added unique deck is silently lost. The fix requires a separate `last_reviewed_deck` variable updated on every card review.

findings.title: vet command uses non-recursive glob; subdirectory YAML files ingested but never vetted
findings.evidence: In `vet_logic`, when `files_to_process is None`, YAML files are discovered with `source_dir.glob('*.yml')` and `source_dir.glob('*.yaml')` (shallow, single directory). By contrast, `load_and_process_flashcard_yamls` in `flashcore/parser.py:286-287` uses `config.source_directory.rglob('*.yaml')` and `rglob('*.yml')` (recursive). Any YAML deck file located in a subdirectory of the source directory will be picked up and ingested by the `ingest` command but will be silently skipped by the `vet` command, meaning its cards will never receive UUID assignment or format normalization.

findings.title: Database connection opened in review_logic but never closed (resource leak)
findings.evidence: In `review_logic`, `db_manager = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path)` is created and `db_manager.initialize_schema()` is called, which implicitly opens a DuckDB connection via `ConnectionHandler.get_connection()`. Neither `db_manager.close_connection()` nor a context-manager exit is ever called; the function just calls `start_review_flow(manager, tags=tags)` and returns. The DuckDB connection leaks until the Python GC finalizes the object. In contrast, `review_all_logic` at `flashcore/cli/_review_all_logic.py:31` correctly uses `with FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path) as db_manager:` ensuring prompt closure.

findings.title: backup_database uses naive datetime (no UTC) producing timezone-dependent filenames
findings.evidence: `backup_database` generates the backup filename using `datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')` (naive local time). Every other timestamp in the codebase uses `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` (e.g., `models.py:97-101`, `session_manager.py:148`). The `find_latest_backup` function at `db_utils.py:248` identifies the most recent backup by lexicographic filename sort. On a host running in a UTC+ timezone, a backup created at local midnight would sort earlier than a backup created at UTC midnight despite occurring later in wall-clock time. This inconsistency can cause `restore` to select a stale backup if the DB is moved across timezones or if CI/CD runs in a different TZ from a developer's machine.

findings.title: check_for_secrets tests back field before front field, inverting error-reporting priority
findings.evidence: In `check_for_secrets`, the loop at line 114 first calls `pattern.search(back)` and only if that doesn't match calls `pattern.search(front)`. When a secret pattern matches both the front (`q`) and the back (`a`) of a card, the returned `YAMLProcessingError` always names `field_name='a'` (line 117), hiding the fact that the front also contains the pattern. Additionally, the function's docstring parameter list omits `front` and `back` as named parameters (they are positional arguments to the function but the docstring only describes `context`), which is a secondary correctness gap. The natural priority for a Q&A flashcard is to check the question text first.

findings.title: _process_file_wrapper extends all_cards before raising on fail_fast, leaving shared list partially mutated
findings.evidence: In `_process_file_wrapper`, when `config.fail_fast=True` and a file produces both valid cards and errors, the sequence is: (1) `all_cards.extend(cards)` adds the valid cards to the caller's list, (2) `all_errors.extend(errors)`, then (3) `raise errors[0]`. Any caller that catches the `YAMLProcessingError` will see `all_cards` partially populated with cards from the failing file, while `all_errors` contains the error. In the current top-level call site (`load_and_process_flashcard_yamls`, `parser.py:299-305`) the exception propagates without being caught, so the partial state never escapes; but any future caller that wraps the loop in a try/except will silently receive an inconsistent card list.

findings.title: Session.end_session() total_duration_ms immediately overwritten by active_duration_ms
findings.evidence: `SessionManager.end_session()` at line 337 calls `self.current_session.end_session()`, which internally sets `self.total_duration_ms = self.calculate_duration()` (gross wall-clock duration, `models.py:361-362`). Line 338 then immediately overwrites it with `self.current_session.total_duration_ms = active_duration_ms` (gross minus pauses). The gross-duration value set by `Session.end_session()` is computed but never used; if pause tracking is broken (e.g., `total_pause_duration_ms` is incorrect), the overwrite silently propagates the wrong value.
