The redundant computation also risks a narrow race if the session object is ever accessed concurrently between lines 337 and 338.

findings.title: timedelta not imported in conftest.py but used in two shared fixtures
findings.evidence: Line 5 imports `from datetime import date, datetime, timezone`; `timedelta` is absent. Lines 180 and 202 call `timedelta(days=5)` and `timedelta(days=10)` inside `sample_review1` and `sample_review2_for_card1` fixtures. Every test that depends on either fixture raises `NameError: name 'timedelta' is not defined` at collection time, silently disabling the entire dependent test surface.

findings.title: Asymmetric None/NaN check raises TypeError when exactly one stab_before is None
findings.evidence: Lines 143-148: the first branch (`is None and is None`) passes when both values are None; the `elif` at line 145 then calls `math.isnan(review1.stab_before)` without guarding against None. If one value is None and the other is NaN the first branch is False and `math.isnan(None)` raises `TypeError: must be real number, not NoneType`, crashing the test on a valid asymmetric input instead of reporting an assertion failure.

findings.title: Exception-aborted review counted as successfully reviewed in session summary
findings.evidence: Test `test_review_all_logic_with_review_error` (line 164) sets `mock_submit.side_effect = Exception('Database error')`. Line 190 then asserts `'Review session complete! Reviewed 1 cards.'`. This validates a production logic defect in `flashcore/cli/_review_all_logic.py:65` where `reviewed_count += 1` fires unconditionally at loop entry before the try/except block (lines 76-103), so any card whose submission raises an exception is still tallied as reviewed in the summary message.

findings.title: Brittle exact logger.error call_count assertion couples test to logging implementation details
findings.evidence: Line 83: `assert mock_logger_error.call_count == 2`. The assertion hardcodes the total number of `logger.error` invocations across the entire `initialize_schema` error-and-rollback-failure path. Any future change that adds, removes, or consolidates an error log call will break this assertion with a misleading count mismatch rather than a meaningful diagnostic message.

findings.title: Path Traversal via startswith Prefix Confusion in Media Path Validation
findings.evidence: validate_single_media_path checks containment with `if not str(full_media_path).startswith(str(abs_assets_root))`. If abs_assets_root resolves to /app/assets, a path like ../assets_extra/evil.bin resolves (via resolve(strict=False)) to /app/assets_extra/evil.bin, which passes the startswith check because the string /app/assets_extra starts with /app/assets. The correct guard is Path.is_relative_to() (Python 3.9+) or appending os.sep to the prefix before comparing. The bug allows a crafted relative media path to reference files in sibling directories outside the intended assets root.

findings.title: SQL Injection in dump_table Public Function
findings.evidence: dump_table(conn, table_name, out_path) executes `conn.execute(f"SELECT * FROM {table_name}")` with no validation or escaping of the table_name parameter. The internal caller dump_database passes only hardcoded table names ('cards','reviews','sessions'), but the function is public and exported with no guard. A direct call with a crafted table_name such as `cards; DROP TABLE cards; --` would execute arbitrary SQL. The # noqa: S608 comment acknowledges the bandit warning but suppresses it without adding a guard.

findings.title: Supply Chain Risk: Unpinned Mutable Git Dependency Installed in CI Runner
findings.evidence: The step `pip install "aiv-protocol @ git+https://github.com/ImmortalDemonGod/aiv-protocol.git"` installs from the default branch HEAD with no pinned SHA or tag. The subsequent step at line 37 provides GITHUB_TOKEN to the installed module via env. Any commit pushed to the aiv-protocol default branch executes code inside the CI runner with access to the GITHUB_TOKEN and the checked-out repository. The same unpinned dependency also appears in pyproject.toml:43 under the [aiv] optional extras group.

findings.title: End-of-Life Python Base Image; Container Runs as Root; No .dockerignore
findings.evidence: FROM python:3.7-slim uses Python 3.7, which reached end-of-life June 2023 and receives no further security patches. Additionally pyproject.toml:12 requires python_requires>=3.10, so pip install . in the container would fail at the metadata check, meaning the container as written is also non-functional. There is no USER directive, so the container runs as root by default. COPY . /app (line 2) copies the full working tree; without a .dockerignore any locally-present .env file (which .gitignore excludes but does not prevent from existing) would be baked into the image layer.

findings.title: sed Metacharacter Injection and Unquoted Variables in rename_project.sh
findings.evidence: Lines 26-29 run `sed -i "s/$original_author/$author/g" $filename` and analogous commands for $name, $urlname, $description. The replacement values $author/$name/$urlname/$description are inserted verbatim as the sed replacement string without escaping. A value containing the sed delimiter `/` (e.g., -a 'foo/bar') splits the substitution command into extra fields, producing a broken sed invocation. A value containing `&` injects the matched text, and `\` injects back-references, corrupting file content. Additionally $filename is unquoted, causing word-splitting on filenames with spaces. These values derive from -a/-n/-u/-d CLI arguments which the workflow in rename_project.yml:37 passes without quoting the GHA context expansions.

findings.title: Overly Broad permissions: write-all in rename_project GHA Workflow
findings.evidence: The workflow-level declaration `permissions: write-all` grants the GITHUB_TOKEN every available write scope: contents, issues, pull-requests, packages, deployments, discussions, id-token, pages, and repository-projects. The workflow only needs contents: write (to commit renamed files and force-push). Excess permissions increase the blast radius if any included action (e.g., stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action@v7 at line 39, which is itself not pinned to a SHA) is compromised.

findings.title: Unpinned Third-Party GitHub Actions Allow Tag-Redirection Attacks
findings.evidence: release.yml:29 uses `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` and rename_project.yml:39 uses `stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action@v7`, both pinned to mutable version tags rather than immutable commit SHAs. A maintainer of either upstream action can move the tag to a malicious commit, which executes in the CI runner on the next workflow trigger. SLSA guidance requires pinning to commit SHAs (e.g., @a1b2c3d...) with the tag as a comment. The rename_project workflow runs with write-all permissions (SEC-006), making a compromised action especially damaging.

findings.title: HTML Sanitisation and Secret Detection Modules Not Connected to Main YAML Ingestion Pipeline
findings.evidence: flashcore/yaml_validators.py implements sanitize_card_text() (uses bleach.clean with an allow-list) and check_for_secrets() (regex patterns for API keys, tokens, Stripe keys, Slack tokens, GitHub PATs). However flashcore/parser.py imports only from flashcore.yaml_models and does not import yaml_validators at all. YAMLProcessor._process_single_raw_card() calls only _prepare_card_data() and Card(**card_data), bypassing both sanitisation and secret scanning. Cards ingested via `flashcore ingest` can therefore contain embedded HTML (including script tags if not stripped by other means) and credential-like strings that are stored unsanitised in the DuckDB database.

findings.title: Unquoted GHA Context Expansions in Shell run Step
findings.evidence: The run step passes GHA expressions directly into the shell command without quoting: `.github/rename_project.sh -a ${{ env.REPOSITORY_OWNER }} -n ${{ env.REPOSITORY_NAME }} -u ${{ env.REPOSITORY_URLNAME }} -d "Awesome ${{ env.REPOSITORY_NAME }} created by ${{ env.REPOSITORY_OWNER }}"`. If REPOSITORY_OWNER or REPOSITORY_NAME contains whitespace or shell metacharacters (e.g., a repository named `foo bar` or `foo$(cmd)`), the unexpanded tokens would be word-split or interpreted as shell. GitHub repository names are restricted by GitHub policy but quoting is still required per GHA security hardening guidance.

findings.title: Dependabot Configured Only for GitHub Actions; Python Packages Not Covered
findings.evidence: The dependabot.yml configures automated updates only for package-ecosystem: "github-actions". The Python runtime dependencies (duckdb, pydantic, bleach, PyYAML, etc.) declared in pyproject.toml are not covered by any Dependabot ecosystem entry (pip). Vulnerabilities in those packages will not generate automated pull requests. The aiv-protocol git dependency (pyproject.toml:43 and aiv-guard.yml:33) cannot be pinned by Dependabot in its current git-URL form, compounding SEC-003.

sys.path.insert(0, str(tmpdir)) in autouse=True fixture prepends the test's tmpdir to the module search path for every test in the suite. A test that writes a Python file to tmpdir could shadow real package imports for subsequent imports within the same process.
findings.title: sys.path injection via autouse fixture

findings.evidence: test_validate_single_media_path_skipped verifies that with skip_media_validation=True any path string, including traversal attempts like 'nonexistent/and/invalid.jpg', is returned as a plain Path with no bounds-checking against assets_root_directory. The skip flag is a complete bypass of all path-traversal controls.
findings.title: skip_media_validation flag disables path traversal protection entirely

findings.evidence: test_secrets_detection_flag (lines 344-362) only exercises the skip_secrets_detection=True bypass path. There is no test confirming that when the flag is False (the secure default), a YAML file containing the secret-pattern string defined at line 83 is actually rejected. The affirmative security control path is untested.
findings.title: Secrets-detection security control lacks an affirmative-enforcement test

findings.evidence: backup_database tests at lines 158-177 verify the backup file exists and its name contains 'backup', but no test verifies that a crafted db_path containing traversal sequences (e.g., '../../sensitive') cannot direct the backup file outside the intended backup directory.
findings.title: Backup destination path not tested for directory traversal

findings.evidence: test_resolve_db_path_from_envvar sets FLASHCORE_DB to an arbitrary string and invokes the CLI stats command, but no test verifies that traversal-style values (e.g., '../../etc/shadow') are rejected or sanitized before being used as the database file path.
findings.title: FLASHCORE_DB env var accepted as DB path without traversal validation test

findings.evidence: with tmpdir.as_cwd(): yield in the autouse=True go_to_tmpdir fixture changes the process working directory to tmpdir for every test. Production code that resolves relative paths against cwd may behave differently under the test harness than in production, masking path-traversal or path-confusion vulnerabilities that only manifest when cwd is a predictable or attacker-controlled location.
findings.title: Autouse cwd manipulation may mask relative-path resolution vulnerabilities
review_processor.py module docstring step 2 'Review history fetching' not present in implementation
findings.evidence: Module docstring (lines 7-14) lists 6 steps; step 2 is 'Review history fetching'. The process_review implementation (lines 91-134) has 5 steps and step 2 at line 99-104 is 'Compute next state using scheduler (O(1) with cached card state)' — no review history is fetched. The card's cached FSRS state (stability, difficulty, state) is passed directly to the scheduler without querying past review records. The process_review method's own inline docstring at lines 69-77 also lists only 5 steps with no 'Review history fetching' entry, directly contradicting the module-level docstring. The module docstring was not updated when the design pivoted from history-based to O(1) cached-state scheduling.

findings.title: tasks.json references removed HPE_ARCHIVE path in completed task subtask
findings.evidence: Task 2 ('Migrate Core Data Models') description at line 79 reads: 'Copy HPE_ARCHIVE/flashcore/card.py to flashcore/models.py.' Subtask 1 details at line 92: 'Execute: cp HPE_ARCHIVE/flashcore/card.py flashcore/models.py. This is the foundation data layer.' CLAUDE.md states explicitly: 'HPE_ARCHIVE/ has been removed. Do not recreate it or reference it.' The task status is 'done' (line 85) but its detail fields retain the archived path reference. Any developer re-reading the task history to understand how models.py was created will encounter a stale path that cannot be followed.

findings.title: migrate.py uses SQLite-dialect INSERT OR REPLACE / INSERT OR IGNORE against a DuckDB connection
findings.evidence: Line 122: `sql = f"INSERT OR REPLACE INTO cards ({col_list}) VALUES ({placeholders})"` and line 143: `sql = f"INSERT OR IGNORE INTO sessions ({col_list}) VALUES ({placeholders})"`. These are SQLite dialect extensions. DuckDB's standard upsert syntax is `INSERT INTO ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE SET ...` / `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`. The module docstring (lines 1-17) describes importing data 'into a new Flashcore DuckDB database', and pyproject.toml:14 requires `duckdb>=1.0.0`. DuckDB 1.0.0 release notes do not document `INSERT OR REPLACE`/`INSERT OR IGNORE` as supported syntax; they were added as SQLite compatibility aliases only in later DuckDB versions. The script may silently fail or produce incorrect results on DuckDB versions that do not recognise these keywords.

findings.title: .taskmaster/config.json projectName is generic 'Taskmaster' default, not 'flashcore'
findings.evidence: Line 28: `"projectName": "Taskmaster"`. This is the unchanged generic default from the TaskMaster tool configuration template. All other project-specific fields in this file (task descriptions, dependencies) reference flashcore, but the top-level project name was never updated to 'flashcore'. TaskMaster uses projectName in generated output and reports; leaving it as 'Taskmaster' means generated artifacts are labelled with the tool's name rather than the project's name.

findings.title: init.sh advertises only 'flask' template in a DuckDB flashcard engine project
findings.evidence: init.sh line 13: `echo "Available templates: flask"`. Line 35 constructs the template URL as `https://github.com/rochacbruno/${template}-project-template`, pointing at the rochacbruno Flask web project template when template='flask'. flashcore is a local-first DuckDB-backed spaced-repetition flashcard engine with no web or Flask components. The `make init` target (Makefile line that invokes `./.github/init.sh`) is therefore advertising and fetching a template with zero applicability to this project's domain. This is a leftover from the rochacbruno/python-project-template origin that was never adapted or removed after project initialization.

findings.title: MarshallingError inherits DatabaseError despite marshalling being a data-conversion concern, not a DB operation
findings.evidence: Line 45: `class MarshallingError(DatabaseError)`. MarshallingError docstring (lines 46-47): 'Indicates an error during data conversion between application models and DB format.' DatabaseError (line 4) represents failures in DB connections, schema setup, and CRUD operations. A caller catching `DatabaseError` will also catch `MarshallingError` even though marshalling failures (wrong data type, missing field, model validation error) are distinct from database connectivity or operation failures and require different recovery strategies. All peer exception classes — `DatabaseConnectionError`, `SchemaInitializationError`, `CardOperationError`, `ReviewOperationError`, `SessionOperationError` — represent genuine DB-tier failures; `MarshallingError` represents an application-layer data-translation failure and should inherit from `ValueError` or a standalone base class. The mismatch means callers cannot distinguish DB-unavailable errors from model-conversion errors via exception type alone.

findings.title: Module docstring asserts unresolved gaps that test bodies assert are FIXED
findings.evidence: Module docstring (lines 1-13) states: 'ReviewSessionManager doesn't create Session objects', 'No session lifecycle management', 'No session analytics'. But test_review_session_manager_now_creates_session_objects (line 59) asserts 'session_from_db is not None  # Gap is FIXED!' and test_review_workflows_now_have_session_integration (line 96) asserts analytics ARE tracked with comment '# Analytics are tracked!'. The module's stated premise (unfixed gaps) directly contradicts the assertions inside the same file. A reader trusting the module docstring would believe the feature is absent; the tests prove it is present.

findings.title: TestRatingSystemDocumentation class documents superseded 0-3 UI scale as 'current behavior'
findings.evidence: TestRatingSystemDocumentation.test_current_rating_scale_mapping (line 321) defines ui_scale = {0: 'Again', 1: 'Hard', 2: 'Good', 3: 'Easy'} and documents a 'Conversion formula: DB = UI + 1'. The rest of the file (class TestRatingSystemInconsistency) asserts the unified 1-4 scale is already in place and the bug is FIXED. The class name ('Documentation') and its docstring imply it describes current production behavior, but it actually codifies the pre-fix state. Any maintainer reading the class will believe the 0-3 UI mapping is still active.

findings.title: Module docstring says 'maintenance hazard' while test body concludes 'FIXED'
findings.evidence: Module docstring (lines 1-9) opens with 'Issue: Core review submission logic exists in two places' and 'This creates maintenance hazards and violates DRY principle' — framing the file as exposing an open defect. test_maintenance_hazard_demonstration (lines 213-277) concludes with 'This test documents that the maintenance hazard has been FIXED by consolidating the logic into a shared ReviewProcessor service' and 'assert review1.session_uuid is not None  # Fixed! Now has session UUID'. The module header is a stale problem statement for a problem the tests themselves confirm is resolved.

findings.title: sqlite_master introspection SQL used against DuckDB database
findings.evidence: Lines 87-93: conn.execute("SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'").fetchall(). sqlite_master is a SQLite-only system table that does not exist in DuckDB. DuckDB exposes table names through information_schema.tables. The identical check in test_db.py:362 correctly uses "SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='main'". The test_db_coverage.py test may silently pass if DuckDB tolerates the query as a no-op or it may fail with a catalog error at runtime, masking whether the schema was actually created.

findings.title: timedelta used in conftest fixtures without being imported
findings.evidence: Line 5: 'from datetime import date, datetime, timezone' — timedelta is absent. Lines 181 and 199 in sample_review1 and sample_review2_for_card1 fixtures use timedelta(days=5) and timedelta(days=10) respectively. Any test that exercises these fixtures without independently importing timedelta in its own module will raise NameError: name 'timedelta' is not defined at fixture-setup time. Pytest will report these as errors rather than failures, potentially obscuring other test results.

findings.title: Inline comment claims Rating.Easy corresponds to value 3 in unified 1-4 scale
findings.evidence: Line 502: 'rating = Rating.Easy  # Corresponds to value 3'. In the unified 1-4 FSRS rating scale that the project adopted (Again=1, Hard=2, Good=3, Easy=4), Rating.Easy.value == 4, not 3. Value 3 corresponds to Rating.Good. The wrong comment persists from the old 0-3 UI era or is a copy-paste error. Any test logic that depends on the comment to interpret the expected scheduler output will produce an off-by-one misread.

findings.title: Multiple zero-assertion tests masquerade as feature-gap documentation
findings.evidence: Three test functions contain only setup code and comment annotations but zero assert statements: test_missing_session_lifecycle_management (approx line 154), test_missing_cross_deck_session_analytics (approx line 220), test_missing_session_performance_analytics (approx line 280). Additionally test_missing_session_analytics_features only queries Session model defaults rather than asserting any lifecycle gap. These tests always pass unconditionally regardless of production code state, providing no regression protection while implying coverage via their names.

findings.title: add_review_and_update_card called with Card object where method docstring requires CardState
findings.evidence: Line 336: db.add_review_and_update_card(sample_review, sample_card) passes a Card object as the second positional argument. Line 427 docstring states 'The method expects a CardState enum for the new state'. Lines 392 and 430 pass CardState.Review (an enum member) as the second argument. The inconsistency means either the method signature changed and one call site was not updated, or the docstring is wrong. A reader cannot determine the correct API from the test file alone.

findings.title: Skipped test has empty body — vacuously passes if ever unskipped
findings.evidence: Lines 387-395: test_initialize_schema_force_recreate is decorated with @pytest.mark.skip(reason='Requires testing_mode mechanism that no longer exists in the current implementation'). The body is a single pass statement. If the skip decorator is removed or the mark is overridden with --run-xfail, the test passes trivially with no assertions. The skip reason references a 'testing_mode mechanism' that has been removed, with no indication of whether the underlying behavior should be tested differently.

findings.title: Two OBSOLETE commented-out test functions left as dead code
findings.evidence: Lines 79-91 contain two functions fully commented out and labeled OBSOLETE: '# def test_review_logic_uses_default_db_path():' and '# def test_review_logic_creates_db_directory(tmp_path):'. Each has a docstring comment explaining they are obsolete but neither has been deleted. They add noise to the file and may confuse readers about whether the behaviors they once tested are now covered elsewhere.

findings.title: Test asserts negative duration is acceptable without error — documents silent invalid-state tolerance
findings.evidence: Lines 273-279: test_session_with_end_before_start sets session.end_time to a datetime before session.start_time, then asserts 'duration < 0  # Negative duration indicates error condition'. The comment acknowledges this is an error condition but no exception is expected or raised. The model silently computes and returns a negative duration. The intent of a spaced-repetition session model implies valid temporal ordering; accepting negative durations without raising violates that intent and would corrupt any session analytics that sum durations.

findings.title: Consolidation-requirements tests only assert len() of hardcoded literals — can never fail
findings.evidence: TestReviewLogicConsolidationRequirements (lines 280-349) contains three test methods. All assertions are of the form 'assert len(some_hardcoded_list) == N' where both the list and N are defined in the same test body. For example line 323: 'assert len(required_interface["parameters"]) == 8' where required_interface is constructed inline three lines above. These assertions can only fail if the author edits the hardcoded list without updating the len check — they cannot detect any production-code regression and provide no verification of the actual ReviewProcessor interface.

findings.title: DB connection leak: FlashcardDatabase used without context manager in _review_logic.py
findings.evidence: Line 31: `db_manager = FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path)` — no `with` block, so `__exit__` is never called and the DuckDB connection is never explicitly closed. Contrast with flashcore/cli/_review_all_logic.py:31 which correctly uses `with FlashcardDatabase(db_path=db_path) as db_manager:`.

findings.title: yaml_validators.py is entirely bypassed by the production ingestion path; bleach sanitization, secrets detection, and media validation are never called
findings.evidence: flashcore/parser.py imports only from flashcore.yaml_models — no import of yaml_validators anywhere in production code (grep across flashcore/ confirmed). yaml_validators.py provides sanitize_card_text (bleach HTML), check_for_secrets (regex patterns), validate_media_paths, validate_card_uuid, and run_card_validation_pipeline — none are reachable from the CLI-driven ingestion flow. Module is exercised only by tests/test_yaml_validators.py.

findings.title: Rollback guard uses getattr(conn, 'closed', True) whose default may silently skip rollback if DuckDB connection lacks .closed attribute
findings.evidence: Lines 255, 600, 736 in database.py and line 54 in schema_manager.py use `if conn and not getattr(conn, 'closed', True): conn.rollback()`. Default True means `not True == False`, so rollback is skipped when the attribute is absent. Whether DuckDBPyConnection exposes a .closed attribute at runtime is UNVERIFIED — if it does not, rollbacks are silently dropped on connection errors.

findings.title: release.yml deploy step invokes setup.py which does not exist; project uses pyproject.toml only
findings.evidence: Line 51 runs `python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel`. No setup.py exists in the repository root (project uses pyproject.toml with setuptools build-backend as confirmed in pyproject.toml:1-3). This step will fail at runtime when a release is triggered.

findings.title: _ProcessedCardData dataclass defined in yaml_models.py but never instantiated or referenced in production code

findings.title: in_memory_db fixtures use return instead of yield, providing no teardown guarantee
findings.evidence: Seven fixtures across six files use `return db` (not `yield db`) after `db.initialize_schema()`: test_review_manager.py:106-115, test_review_logic_duplication.py:25-29, test_rating_system_inconsistency.py:33-37, test_review_processor.py:24-28, test_review_processor.py:376-381, test_session_analytics_gaps.py:28-33, test_session_manager.py:22-27. pytest only runs post-yield teardown for generator fixtures; `return` fixtures rely on GC for cleanup, which is not guaranteed within the test session and can cause handle exhaustion in large test runs.
